hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'urbs' — 'Urbem' with -em is accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'urbe' — 'Urbem' is accusative, direct object

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'urbis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'urbis' ends in -is
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'urbis' ends in -is

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'Roma' — 'Romam' with -am is accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'Roma' — 'Romam' is accusative in apposition with 'Urbem'

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'Romae' with -ae is 1st declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'Romae' with -ae is 1st declension
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'Romae' with -ae is 1st declension

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'principium' — 'principio' with -o is ablative after 'a'
hints.dative: While 'principio' could be dative, here it is ablative governed by the preposition 'a'
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'principium' — 'principio' is ablative after 'a'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'principii' with -i is 2nd declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'principii' with -i is 2nd declension
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'principii' with -i is 2nd declension

hints.accusative: While 'reges' can be accusative plural, here it is the subject of 'habuere'
hints.genitive: Genitive singular would be 'regis' — 'reges' is nominative plural
hints.vocative: Livy is narrating, not addressing kings

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'regis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'regis' ends in -is
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'regis' ends in -is

hints.to dwell: That would be 'habitare' — 'habuere' comes from 'habere' (to have/hold)
hints.to consider: While 'habere' can mean 'to consider', its primary meaning is 'to have/hold'
hints.to carry: That would be 'ferre' — 'habuere' comes from 'habere' (to have/hold)

steps.reveal: habuere = they held (= habuerunt, archaic 3rd pl. perfect)

hints.present infinitive: Present infinitive is 'habere' — 'habuere' with -uere is perfect (archaic -ere for -erunt)
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'habebant' — 'habuere' is perfect indicative
hints.pluperfect indicative: Pluperfect would be 'habuerant' — 'habuere' is perfect indicative

hints.habito, habitare, habitavi, habitatum: These are the parts of 'habitare' (to dwell)
hints.haurio, haurire, hausi, haustum: These are the parts of 'haurire' (to draw/drain)
hints.fero, ferre, tuli, latum: These are the parts of 'ferre' (to carry)

translation: The city of Rome, from the beginning, kings held
